text: Objects captured in the same second: neither can claim influence over the other. They are treated as contemporaneous.
Batch imports with synthetic timestamps: use the source document's publication date, not the import timestamp. If no publication date is available, treat the entire batch as contemporaneous.
Retrospective notes on Timeline Nodes: the note's timestamp is when the note was added, not when the event occurred. Causal analysis uses the event's original timestamp.
Filtering edges by type:. Only supports, entailment, and causal edges carry influence. similarity and shared_entity edges do not — being about the same topic is not influence.
Filtering by temporal precedence:. Remove any edge where from_object.created_at >= to_object.created_at. This enforces the causal direction.

Pruning redundant paths (transitive reduction):. If A->B->C and A->C both exist, remove A->C. The direct path A->B->C is more informative because it shows the intermediary. The redundant A->C edge adds visual noise without adding causal information.
Computing structural properties:
Roots: Nodes with in-degree 0. These are the original sources — ideas that entered the system without being influenced by anything already in it.
Leaves: Nodes with out-degree 0. These are terminal ideas — they have not (yet) influenced anything else.
Longest path: The maximum depth of the influence chain. Long paths indicate sustained lines of inquiry.
Branching factor: Average out-degree. High branching means a source spawned many derivatives.
SHA hash: Immutable identifier linking to the exact state at that point
Timestamp: When the event occurred
Actor: Who or what performed the action (user, engine pass, LLM)
Action: What happened (created, modified, promoted, contested)
Delta: What changed (before/after for modifications)
causal_engine.py — The core causal analysis module. Contains:
build_influence_dag() — Filters edges by temporal precedence, prunes redundant paths via transitive reduction. Returns a dict with nodes, edges, roots, and leaves.
trace_lineage() — BFS traversal of the influence DAG in both directions from a seed node.
provenance.py — Provenance tracking and narrative generation. Contains:
trace_provenance() — Returns the complete provenance chain for an object: timeline of events, causal lineage, contradictions, and community membership.
generate_provenance_narrative() — Produces a compact English summary of an object's history and lineage.
engine.py Pass 7 — Causal lineage pass. Runs after claim-level NLI (Pass 6) to establish claim-level causal transfers based on support/entailment edges filtered by temporal precedence.
claim-analysis: NLI scores determine which edges carry evidential weight
graph-theory: The influence DAG is a subgraph of the knowledge graph, built with NetworkX
knowledge-representation: Provenance uses SHA-linked primitives from models.py
graph-theory: Influence DAGs are input to temporal_evolution.py for tracking how influence patterns change over time
knowledge-representation: Provenance narratives become part of an object's metadata
Never create a causal edge that violates temporal precedence.. If from_object.created_at >= to_object.created_at, the edge is invalid. No exceptions.
Never skip transitive reduction.. Redundant paths in influence DAGs obscure the true causal structure and make narratives confusing.

Never treat similarity edges as causal.. Two objects being about the same topic (similarity, shared_entity) does not mean one influenced the other. Only supports, entailment, and causal edges carry influence.
Never bypass _generate_sha() for provenance events.. Architectural invariant #4. Every state must be SHA-traceable.
Never modify Timeline Nodes. (except retrospective_notes). Architectural invariant #3. If you need to annotate past events, use retrospective_notes.
Never traverse more than 15 hops in lineage tracing without explicit user request.. Deep chains are almost always noise beyond 10-15 hops.

Never generate a provenance narrative without checking for contradictions.. If the lineage includes contested claims, the narrative must mention them — hiding contradictions defeats the purpose of provenance.

Never treat batch-imported objects as having causal relationships with each other based on import timestamp.. Use the source document's publication date. If unavailable, treat the batch as contemporaneous.
refs/research_api/apps/notebook/causal_engine.py for influence DAG construction and lineage tracing
refs/research_api/apps/notebook/provenance.py for provenance chains and narrative generation
The Index-API engine.py Pass 7 for claim-level causal transfer
Do not rely on training data for library APIs. The source files contain the real implementation details.
Atomic: one proposition, not a conjunction
Self-contained: understandable without context
Falsifiable: can be checked against evidence
Not a question, command, or meta-statement
claim_decomposition.py — Contains decompose_claims() function. Both LLM-based and rule-based paths. Max 20 claims per text. Read this first for the decomposition logic.
advanced_nlp.py — Contains analyze_pair() function for NLI scoring. Uses CrossEncoder with the HAS_PYTORCH flag for two-mode safety. Read for NLI score interpretation.
engine.py Pass 6 — Claim-level NLI pass. Runs pairwise NLI between claims of related objects. Creates Tension objects for contradictions and support edges for entailments.
compose_engine.py NLI pass — Live NLI scoring during write-time. Shows the user when their new text contradicts or supports existing claims. Stateless.
models.py: Claim model (text, source_object, epistemic_status, confidence), Tension model (claim_a, claim_b, nli_score, resolution_status)
engine.py: Pass 6 orchestrates the claim-level NLI pipeline
compose_engine.py: Real-time NLI as the user writes
provenance.py: Claims carry SHA-linked provenance back to their source object
entailment > 0.8 -> Create support edge (Edge type: supports)
contradiction > 0.7 -> Create Tension object
contradiction > 0.5 and < 0.7 -> Flag for human review
All scores < 0.5 -> Neutral, no action
Never auto-promote claims to accepted status.. Architectural invariant #7: LLMs propose, humans review. The system can auto-detect contested but never auto-accepts.
Never run pairwise NLI on all claims without pre-filtering.. The combinatorial explosion makes this O(n^2). Always filter candidate pairs by object-level similarity first.
Never exceed 20 claims per text.. This is a hard limit. If decomposition produces more, select the most substantive.
Never treat NLI as symmetric.. "A entails B" does not imply "B entails A". Check both directions for contradiction detection.
Never create a Tension without storing the NLI scores.. Tensions must be auditable — the scores explain why the system flagged a contradiction.
Never bypass the two-mode contract.. Claim decomposition must work in production (rule-based fallback). NLI scoring is dev-only unless a production-safe model is deployed.
Never modify compose_engine to write Tension objects to the database.. compose_engine is stateless by architectural invariant #8. It reports contradictions; engine.py persists them.
refs/sentence-transformers/ for CrossEncoder NLI model behavior and score interpretation
The Index-API files listed above for existing decomposition and NLI patterns
Do not rely on training data for library APIs. The refs/ directory contains the real source code.
Fragility 0.01: robust understanding (1% of claims depend on any single source)
Fragility 0.30: fragile (30% of claims collapse if one source is removed)
Fragility varies per Source: some are load-bearing, others are redundant
New file: tms.py (Truth Maintenance System core)
New file: counterfactual.py (simulation engine)
Depends on: Claim model with dependency tracking, provenance.py
Prerequisites: Claims must have populated dependency records
API: new endpoint in views.py for simulation requests
Never simulate on incomplete dependency data.. If <50% of Claims have dependency records, the simulation is unreliable. Warn the user.
Never modify the real graph during simulation.. Simulations are read-only projections.
Never allow circular dependencies.. The dependency graph must be a DAG. Validate on insertion.
Never present fragility scores without context.. 0.30 fragility might be acceptable for early-stage research and alarming for a legal brief.
domain_config.py: per-Notebook/per-cluster engine_config management
Integration with learned scorer: cluster-conditioned feature importances
auto_classify.py: extended with meta-learned classifiers
New management command: analyze_domain_characteristics to profile a cluster
Never apply domain adaptation with fewer than 50 objects in a cluster.. Not enough signal.
Never auto-deploy domain packs.. Human reviews and installs.
Never assume domain boundaries are static.. Clusters evolve as new objects enter.
Never hard-code domain heuristics.. Everything should be learnable from data.
New file: optimizer.py
Library: DEAP for evolutionary algorithms, Optuna for Bayesian + evolutionary
Schedule: periodic optimization runs (weekly or after major data changes)
Output: evolved configs deployed to per-Notebook engine_config
Integration: results feed into domain-specialization (Level 5)
Never optimize without a holdout test set.. Overfitting to the training corpus is easy.
Never run evolution on production data.. Use a snapshot/staging corpus.
Never accept a config that degrades any IQ axis below its pre-evolution score.. Pareto improvement only.
Never use more than 50 generations without convergence checks.. Diminishing returns after ~20-30 generations.
Never train a GNN on fewer than 100 nodes.. Results are meaningless below this scale.
Never use more than 3 GNN layers.. Over-smoothing causes all embeddings to converge. 2 layers is standard for knowledge graphs.
Never skip negative sampling.. Link prediction requires 5-10x negative edges per positive edge. Random negative sampling is fine to start.
Never leak test edges into training.. Edge splits must be strictly temporal or random, never overlapping.
Never run GNN training in production.. This is a Modal GPU job only. Two-mode contract: production has no PyTorch.
Never replace KGE with GNN.. They capture different structural signals. KGE (RotatE) operates on individual triples; GNN operates on neighborhoods. Both should be features in the learned scorer.
Never forget GraphSAGE for new objects.. When a new Object is captured, GraphSAGE can embed it immediately from neighbors. R-GCN requires retraining.
alpha = 0.85 — Damping factor. Probability of following a link vs teleporting. Standard value.
max_iter = 100 — Usually converges in 20-30 iterations.
tol = 1e-06 — Convergence threshold.
Graph partitioning (spectral clustering)
Measuring graph connectivity (algebraic connectivity = lambda_2)
Dimensionality reduction for graph visualization
graph.py — Core graph construction. Builds a bipartite NetworkX graph from Objects and Edges. Computes PageRank with 5-minute cache. Read this first for the graph data model.
community.py — Louvain community detection via detect_communities(). Persists community assignments. Read for the community detection pipeline.
gap_analysis.py — Structural hole detection between communities. Uses Burt's constraint to find brokerage opportunities.
causal_engine.py — build_influence_dag() constructs temporal influence DAGs. Filters by temporal precedence, prunes redundant paths. Returns nodes, edges, roots, and leaves.
temporal_evolution.py — Sliding-window graph dynamics. Tracks how communities, centrality, and connectivity change over time windows.
self_organize.py — Uses community detection output for 5 feedback loops: classify, cluster-to-notebook, entity promotion, edge decay, and emergent types.
PageRank: 5-minute cache in graph.py. PageRank is expensive on large graphs but changes slowly. Cache invalidation happens on edge creation/deletion.
Communities: Persisted to database via persist_communities(). Recomputed on demand or when edge count changes significantly.
Object classification (which community should a new object join?)
Notebook organization (should a community become its own notebook?)
Entity promotion (are community-central entities canonical?)
Edge decay (do weak inter-community edges deserve to exist?)
Emergent types (does a community represent a new ObjectType?)
Never build a graph without checking for isolated nodes.. Isolated nodes (degree 0) should be flagged, not silently included — they skew centrality measures.
Never use unweighted PageRank on a weighted graph.. Edge confidence is the weight. Ignoring weights treats a 0.3-confidence edge the same as a 0.95-confidence edge.
Never skip transitive reduction on influence DAGs.. Without it, the DAG is unreadable and the root/leaf analysis is misleading.
Never modify the from_object/to_object convention.. Graph construction depends on this. Architectural invariant #2.
Never run community detection on graphs with fewer than 10 nodes.. The results are meaningless at that scale. Return a single community.
Never cache PageRank without a cache invalidation strategy.. The 5-minute TTL in graph.py exists for a reason. If you add new caching, define when it invalidates.
Never use the Fiedler vector on disconnected graphs.. The algebraic connectivity is 0 for disconnected graphs, and the Fiedler vector is undefined. Check connectivity first.
Never mutate the NetworkX graph object in place during analysis.. Build the graph, analyze it, discard it. Graph construction is cheap; accidental mutation bugs are expensive.
refs/networkx/ for NetworkX graph construction, algorithms, and centrality functions
The Index-API files listed above for existing graph patterns and caching strategies

k1 = 1.5 — Controls term frequency saturation. Higher values let repeated terms keep boosting the score. At k1=0, BM25 reduces to binary (present/absent). At k1=1.5, a term appearing 3 times scores ~2.4x a single occurrence, not 3x.
b = 0.75 — Controls document length normalization. At b=1.0, short documents are heavily favored. At b=0.0, length is ignored. 0.75 is the classic sweet spot.
IDF(q) = log((N - n(q) + 0.5) / (n(q) + 0.5) + 1) where N = total docs, n(q) = docs containing q.
Synonym injection: Add known synonyms from the knowledge graph (Entity edges of type similarity).
PRF (Pseudo-Relevance Feedback): Take terms from top-3 BM25 results, add to query, re-search.
LLM expansion: Ask the LLM to generate related terms (expensive, use sparingly).
bm25.py — BM25 lexical index. Read this before modifying any text search path. Handles tokenization, index building, and querying.
vector_store.py — FAISS index management. Stores both SBERT embeddings and KGE embeddings. Read for index creation, insertion, and search patterns.
engine.py Passes 3-5 — Post-capture enrichment where retrieval happens:
Pass 3: TF-IDF similarity (production mode fallback)
Pass 4: FAISS vector similarity (local/dev mode)
Pass 5: SBERT semantic similarity (local/dev mode)
compose_engine.py — Live write-time discovery. Uses retrieval to find related objects as the user types. Stateless: text in, suggestions out, no DB writes.
BM25 (pure Python, rank-bm25 library)
TF-IDF via scikit-learn
FAISS indexes (requires faiss-cpu or faiss-gpu)
SBERT embeddings (requires sentence-transformers + PyTorch)
Cross-encoder re-ranking
Always check the HAS_PYTORCH / feature-available flags before calling FAISS or SBERT paths. Never import torch at module level.
Never import faiss or torch at module level.. Always use lazy imports guarded by try/except with a _FEATURE_AVAILABLE flag.
Never assume IndexFlatIP is the right index.. Check corpus size and choose accordingly.
Never skip BM25.. Even when FAISS is available, lexical search catches exact matches that embeddings miss. Always include BM25 in a fusion pipeline.
Never hardcode k1 or b.. These should be configurable, ideally per-notebook via engine_config.
Never build a FAISS index without checking if one already exists.. Index creation is expensive; always check for a persisted index first.
Never return raw scores to the user without context.. BM25 scores, cosine similarities, and RRF scores are on different scales. Normalize or explain.
Never modify compose_engine to write to the database.. It is stateless by architectural invariant.
The Index-API files listed above for existing patterns
Symmetry: supports(A, B) and supports(B, A) when the relation rotation is 0 or pi
Antisymmetry: entailment(A, B) but not entailment(B, A) when rotation is non-trivial
Composition: A influences B + B influences C -> A influences C via rotation composition
Inversion: supports(A, B) implies supported_by(B, A) via conjugate rotation
Embedding dimension: 128 (good tradeoff between expressiveness and compute)
Epochs: 100-500 depending on graph size
Negative sampling: Corrupt head or tail with ratio 1:5
Optimizer: Adam, lr=0.001
Regularization: L2 weight decay
models.py — All epistemic primitives defined as Django models. This is the ground truth for what the system can represent. Read before adding or modifying any primitive.
vector_store.py — KGE embeddings stored and queried via FAISS. Contains the bridge between PyKEEN training output and runtime inference.
scripts/train_kge.py — RotatE training pipeline. Reads the knowledge graph, constructs triples, trains embeddings, exports to FAISS index.
Never use source/target for edge endpoints.. The system uses from_object / to_object. This is architectural invariant #2.
Never create an edge without a human-readable reason.. This is architectural invariant #1. "similarity: 0.85" is not a reason. "Both discuss the limitations of BM25 for long documents" is a reason.
Never auto-promote extracted knowledge to canonical status.. Architectural invariant #7. LLMs propose, humans review.
Never bypass _generate_sha() for provenance.. Architectural invariant #4. Every primitive must be SHA-traceable.
Never modify Timeline Nodes after creation. (except retrospective_notes). Architectural invariant #3.
Never hard-delete objects.. Use soft-delete (is_deleted=True). Architectural invariant #6.
Never add an epistemic primitive without defining its status lifecycle.. Every primitive needs a clear progression from captured to canonical.
Never train KGE on a graph with fewer than 100 triples.. The embeddings will be meaningless. Check graph size before training.
refs/pykeen/ for PyKEEN RotatE training, scoring functions, and embedding export
The Index-API models.py for the current data model
The Index-API scripts/train_kge.py for the training pipeline
Qwen 2.5 0.5B: Smallest viable model. Fast to fine-tune. Good for structured tasks.
Phi-3 Mini (3.8B): Better generation quality. Still fits on a single A100 with LoRA.
Mistral 7B: Best quality/size ratio if you can afford it.
Faithfulness: Does the output contradict any graph node? (NLI check against cited sources)
Coverage: Does it use the available evidence? (% of provided context referenced)
Novelty: Does it propose something not already explicit? (SBERT similarity to existing claims)
Traceability: Can every assertion be linked to a specific graph path? (citation accuracy)

Inference: API endpoint for graph-conditioned generation

Never generate without graph grounding.. Every prompt must include specific graph content as context.
Never evaluate with BLEU alone.. Faithfulness and traceability matter more than fluency.
Never fine-tune on fewer than 1,000 examples.. Below this, the model overfits to training patterns.
Never auto-promote generated hypotheses.. Invariant #7: LLMs propose, humans review.
Never run LM inference in Railway production.. Modal GPU only.
User clicks a connection in Map or Inspector
User navigates from Object A to Object B via connection
User adds a note referencing both objects
User creates a manual edge
User deletes an auto-generated edge
User marks "not useful" (new UI affordance)
Connection presented but never clicked in N days (strength 0.3)
Search web for co-occurrence of key terms from both objects
Authoritative source confirms relationship: positive
Zero relevant results: weak negative (strength 0.3)
Both objects in same manually-created Notebook: positive
Both objects share a user-created tag: positive
Human-written note explicitly links them: strong positive (1.0)
Works with hundreds (not millions) of labeled pairs
Handles mixed types (floats, ints, bools, categoricals) natively
Produces feature importance scores (which signals matter most?)
Trains in seconds, not hours
Interpretable (explain why a connection scored high)
No GPU needed

Never train without cross-validation.. Small datasets overfit easily. Always 5-fold CV.
Never deploy a scorer without the graceful fallback.. The <50 / 50-200 / 200+ thresholds protect the system.
Never snapshot feature vectors after the fact.. Features must be captured at the moment the connection is shown, not reconstructed later (model parameters may have changed).
Never use neural networks until you have 10,000+ labels.. GBTs are strictly better for small datasets.
Never forget the two-mode contract.. Scikit-learn ships in production. PyTorch does not. The scorer must work with sklearn.
Never train without running IQ Tracker before and after.. Every training run must produce measurable improvement.
Never blend learned and fixed scores without logging both.. For debugging, always record both the learned score and the fixed-weight score.
Traverses the graph for supporting Sources, entailment edges, corroborating Claims
Constructs the strongest possible case FOR the Claim
Must cite specific Objects and Edges (no free-form speculation)
Searches for contradiction edges, conflicting Claims, missing evidence
Identifies assumptions that are not explicitly supported
Must cite specific Objects and Edges
Weighs the quality and quantity of evidence from both sides
Identifies where the Advocate and Critic agree (settled) vs disagree (epistemically interesting)
Produces a structured verdict: confidence (0-1), key supporting evidence, key weaknesses, unresolved questions
Human agrees with verdict: positive label for Judge's reasoning pattern
Human disagrees: negative label, human's correction becomes ground truth
Over time, all three agents improve from human oversight

Output: confidence scores, evidence lists, disagreement flags
Integration: disagreements surfaced in Resurface as "epistemically interesting"
Training: human review of Judge verdicts feeds back via ConnectionFeedback
Never let agents argue from information outside the graph.. Grounding is non-negotiable.
Never auto-accept Judge verdicts.. Invariant #7: humans review.
Never run all three agents synchronously in a request.. Debate is a background task (RQ job).
Never deploy debate without the Judge.. Advocate-only or Critic-only produces biased assessments.
Never skip the disagreement surface.. The most valuable output is where agents disagree, not where they agree.

Never replace text extraction with VLM for simple text documents.. VLMs are expensive. Use them for documents where layout matters (tables, figures, multi-column).
Never run VLM inference in production.. Modal GPU only.
Never assume VLM output is correct.. Visual extraction needs human review (Invariant #7).
Never break existing text embeddings when adding visual features.. Cross-modal embeddings are additive.

Semantic similarity scoring for edge creation
embeddings.py — spaCy 300-dimensional GloVe vectors. Production-mode embedding path. No PyTorch required. Used for basic similarity when SBERT is unavailable.
advanced_nlp.py — SBERT + CrossEncoder NLI integration. Contains the HAS_PYTORCH flag and lazy loading pattern. Read this carefully — it is the canonical example of the two-mode pattern.
adaptive_ner.py — PhraseMatcher per ObjectType with 30-minute cache TTL. Graph-learned entity patterns using Aho-Corasick matching.
engine.py Pass 1 — NER extraction pass. Runs spaCy NER on newly captured objects, creates entity edges.
engine.py Pass 5 — SBERT semantic similarity pass. Computes pairwise similarity between objects using sentence embeddings. Only runs in local/dev mode.
Never import torch, sentence_transformers, or transformers at module level.. Always use the try/except pattern with a feature flag.
Never load a transformer model at import time.. Use the lazy loading pattern shown above.
Never assume SBERT is available.. Every code path must have a spaCy or TF-IDF fallback that produces usable (if lower-quality) results.
Never forget the E5 instruction prefix.. If switching to an E5 model, all encoding calls must prepend "query: " or "passage: " as appropriate.
Never modify the PhraseMatcher cache TTL without understanding the performance implications.. 30 minutes is calibrated to balance freshness against rebuild cost.
Never add a new ObjectType without updating the NER entity-type mapping.. The mapping between spaCy labels and ObjectTypes must stay in sync.
Never run cross-encoder scoring on more than ~200 pairs at once.. Cross-encoders are O(n) per pair with large constant factors. Batch and paginate.
refs/spacy/ for spaCy pipeline internals, PhraseMatcher, and entity recognition
refs/sentence-transformers/ for SBERT encoding, cross-encoder scoring, and model loading
The Index-API files listed above for existing patterns and the two-mode contract
Beta(alpha, beta) where alpha represents confirming interactions and beta represents absence or contradiction.
User engagement (clicking, confirming, extending an edge) increments alpha.
Absence of interaction over time increments beta (time-based decay).
Expected edge strength = alpha / (alpha + beta).
This means edges start strong after creation and weaken without reinforcement, but a single re-engagement can restore strength because it shifts the posterior.
Recency: Recent evidence counts more. Use exponential decay, not hard cutoffs.
Source reliability: Track per-source accuracy over time. A source that frequently produces contradicted claims gets down-weighted.
Corroboration count: Independent sources confirming the same claim strengthen it superlinearly (not just additively).
Contradiction count: Contradictions don't cancel support; they increase uncertainty (widen the confidence interval).
Support graph: Edges from evidence that confirms the model's claims.
Attack graph: Edges from evidence that contradicts or undermines the model.
Net score is not support minus attack. Use Bayesian belief network propagation where attack evidence increases entropy rather than simply subtracting.
Never produce point estimates without intervals:
Edge strength: report as [lower, expected, upper] at a stated credible interval (e.g., 90%).
Retrieval scores: report calibrated confidence, not raw cosine similarity.
Model scores: report as distributions, not single numbers.
When confidence is low, say so explicitly. A wide interval is more honest than a precise wrong number.
At 0.0: Only high-confidence, well-corroborated connections. Tight thresholds. Fewer but more reliable edges.
At 1.0: Speculative connections included. Loose thresholds. More edges but higher false-positive rate.
The dial interpolates between conservative and aggressive parameter sets for every engine pass.

self_organize.py -- evolve_edges(): Implements exponential decay with 60-day half-life. Edges below MIN_EDGE_STRENGTH (0.05) are pruned. Each pruning event is recorded in the Timeline for auditability. The decay formula: new_strength = old_strength * exp(-lambda * days_since_last_interaction) where lambda = ln(2) / 60.

resurface.py: Currently uses static signal weights for resurfacing stale-but-important objects. Planned upgrade to Personalized PageRank (PPR)-based scoring would weight signals dynamically based on user engagement patterns. When working here, preserve the static fallback path.

engine.py -- interpolate_config(): The Novelty Dial. Takes a float 0.0-1.0 and interpolates between conservative and aggressive parameter dictionaries. Every engine pass reads its thresholds from this interpolated config. Changes here affect all 7 passes.
All probabilistic parameters are stored in engine_config per Notebook, not hardcoded.
Decay and pruning actions always produce Timeline events with before/after values.
Confidence scores in API responses include the uncertainty band, not just the point estimate.
Scoring functions degrade gracefully: if SBERT is unavailable, fall back to TF-IDF cosine with wider uncertainty bands.
Never present weak evidence as high confidence.. If the credible interval is wide, the output must reflect that. Do not collapse a Beta(2, 8) into "20% confidence" without showing the interval.

Never silently change scoring priors.. Any change to decay rates, half-lives, minimum thresholds, or interpolation boundaries requires a migration note and Timeline event. These are not internal tuning knobs -- they affect what users see.

Never remove deterministic fallback scoring.. The production environment cannot run SBERT. Every probabilistic scoring path must have a deterministic fallback (TF-IDF, BM25, or rule-based) that produces reasonable results.
Never hardcode thresholds.. All thresholds (MIN_EDGE_STRENGTH, decay half-life, novelty dial bounds) come from engine_config. If you find a hardcoded threshold, make it configurable.

Never conflate absence with contradiction.. An edge that has not been interacted with is stale, not wrong. Decay reduces confidence; contradiction increases uncertainty. These are different operations on different parameters.
Not Turing-complete. Methods are declarative specifications, not general-purpose programs. They describe what to check, score, or extract -- not arbitrary computation.
Declarative over imperative. A Method says "these conditions must hold" or "score these dimensions," not "loop through items and if X then Y."
Structured JSON storage. Method definitions are stored as structured JSON in the database, not as raw code strings. This makes them inspectable, versionable, and safe to execute.
Domain-scoped. Each Method operates within a specific domain (benchmarking, compliance, scoring, extraction). The DSL vocabulary is scoped to that domain.
Each version is a snapshot of the Method definition at a point in time.
Versions carry provenance: who created them, what source material informed them, what previous version they derive from.
Version diffs are meaningful because the structured JSON format makes changes inspectable.
Rolling back to a previous version is always possible because versions are immutable snapshots.
Records input (what was evaluated), output (what the Method produced), and provenance (which Method version, when, by whom).
MethodRun outcomes feed back into learning: if a Method consistently produces results that users override, the Method needs revision.
Duration tracking enables performance monitoring: Methods that take too long need optimization or decomposition.
definition: Structured JSON storing the Method's rules, scoring dimensions, or checklist items.
version: Integer version number, auto-incremented on save.
provenance: Links to source Objects that informed the Method's design.
linked_objects: Objects this Method can operate on or was derived from.
sha_hash: SHA-based identity for provenance tracking. Generated by _generate_sha() -- never bypass this.
method: ForeignKey to the Method (with version pinning).
input: JSON recording what was evaluated.
output: JSON recording what the Method produced.
status: Enum tracking run state (pending, running, completed, failed).
duration: TimeDelta recording execution time.
sha_hash: SHA-based identity, same provenance contract as Method.
Pick one domain (e.g., benchmark protocols for ML papers) and make it work end-to-end.
Resist the urge to generalize the DSL before you have three working domain implementations.
The JSON definition schema should be validated on save, not just on execution.

Never make the DSL Turing-complete.. If you find yourself adding loops, conditionals, or variable assignment to the Method definition schema, stop. Methods are declarative. If the logic is too complex for a declarative spec, it belongs in a Python function that the Method references, not in the DSL itself.
Never bypass _generate_sha().. Method and MethodRun identity depends on SHA hashes for provenance. Manually setting or skipping the hash breaks the provenance chain.
Never execute untrusted Method definitions without validation.. The JSON definition must be schema-validated before execution. A malformed definition should fail at save time, not at run time.
Never discard MethodRun history.. Runs are the empirical record of what a Method actually does. Even failed runs carry diagnostic value. Soft-delete only.
Never generalize before specializing.. The DSL should work perfectly in one domain before being extended to a second. Premature abstraction produces a DSL that is adequate for nothing.
Never store raw code strings in Method definitions.. The definition is structured JSON, not eval-able code. If you need custom logic, reference a named function, do not inline it.
Observe context. : cluster properties (size, density, domain, object types)
Choose action. : engine configuration (pass weights, thresholds)
Observe reward. : user engagement with resulting connections
Update policy. : adjust configuration preferences for this context
State: current node + path history
Action: choose which edge to follow
Reward: +1 if the agent reaches a target node, -small_penalty per step
Policy: neural network that scores (state, action) pairs
Immediate: user clicked a connection (binary)
Short-term: user wrote a note referencing the connection (within session)
Long-term: IQ axis improvement (weekly measurement)
Level 5: adaptive_engine.py with contextual bandit for per-cluster config
Level 7+: path_reasoner.py with MINERVA-style multi-hop agent
Training: engagement events as rewards, updated via RQ periodic task
Exploration: Thompson sampling (bandits) or epsilon-greedy (MINERVA)
Never use full RL when contextual bandits suffice.. Level 5 does not need sequential decision-making.
Never deploy without an exploration strategy.. Pure exploitation converges to local optima.
Never use raw click counts as rewards.. Normalize by impression count. A connection shown 100 times with 5 clicks is worse than one shown 10 times with 3 clicks.
Never train the path reasoner on graphs with fewer than 500 nodes.. RL needs sufficient state space.
Detection. : Identify a structural signal (e.g., repeated entity mentions, growing cluster, stale edge).
Proposal. : Generate a candidate action (e.g., "promote entity X to Object", "create Notebook from cluster Y").
Threshold. : Check quantitative gates before acting (e.g., minimum mention count, minimum modularity score, minimum cluster size).
Mutation. : Execute the structural change (create Object, create Notebook, prune Edge).
Timeline event. : Record what changed, why, and what the before/after state looked like.
Louvain maximizes modularity: the fraction of edges within communities minus the expected fraction if edges were random.
A modularity score >= 0.15 indicates meaningful community structure (not random grouping).
Communities of 5+ objects with >30% unassigned objects trigger Notebook creation.
The algorithm is non-deterministic (depends on node processing order), so results may vary across runs. This is acceptable -- the threshold gates prevent spurious Notebooks.
Adaptive NER (PhraseMatcher-based) scans incoming Objects for entity mentions.
When the same entity appears in 5+ Objects and no corresponding Object exists, the loop proposes promotion.
Promotion creates a new Object of the appropriate type and creates Edges to each mentioning Object.
This is a feedback loop: promoted entities become PhraseMatcher patterns, which improves future entity detection.
A cluster of 8+ objects where >70% are Notes sharing common properties suggests a new ObjectType.
The system proposes (not auto-creates) the new type, because type creation affects the entire schema.
This loop runs in suggestion-only mode: it records the proposal but does not mutate without human review.
Assigns ObjectTypes to Objects based on content analysis.
Uses heuristics (length, structure, metadata presence) and optionally LLM classification.
Non-destructive: only sets type on untyped Objects. Never overrides human-assigned types.
Delegates type-specific logic to auto_classify.py.
Runs Louvain community detection on the Object-Edge graph.
Communities meeting the threshold (5+ objects, modularity >= 0.15, >30% unassigned) trigger Notebook creation.
Non-destructive: Objects are added to the new Notebook without removing them from existing Notebooks.
The Notebook gets a generated name from the cluster's most central terms.
Adaptive NER scans Objects for entity mentions.
Entities appearing in 5+ Objects with no existing Object representation are promoted.
Creates the Object and Edges with edge_type="mentions" and reason explaining the promotion.
This is a feedback loop: the new Object becomes a PhraseMatcher pattern for future NER runs.
Exponential decay with 60-day half-life applied to all auto-generated edges.
Edges below MIN_EDGE_STRENGTH (0.05) are pruned.
Each pruning event is recorded in the Timeline with before/after strength values.
Human-created edges are exempt from decay.
Scans clusters for homogeneous composition (8+ objects, >70% same base type, shared properties).
Produces a suggestion record, not an automatic type creation.
The suggestion includes: proposed type name, shared properties, member Object count, confidence score.
organize_batch(): Called for newly ingested Objects. Runs loops 1-3 (classify, cluster, promote). Lightweight and safe to run on every ingestion.
periodic_reorganize(): Runs nightly via RQ scheduled task. Executes all 5 loops. Heavier but bounded by thresholds.
preview_*() functions: Non-mutating versions of each loop. Return what would change without changing it. Every mutating loop must have a corresponding preview function.
auto_classify.py: Type-specific classification rules. Contains the heuristic decision tree for ObjectType assignment.
community.py: Louvain community detection implementation. Wraps networkx community detection with Index-API-specific graph construction.

Never auto-promote to canonical knowledge without review.. Entity promotion creates Objects and Edges, but these are marked as auto-generated. They appear in the review queue, not in the canonical graph, until a human confirms them.
Never mutate without dry-run visibility.. Every mutating loop must have a preview_* counterpart. If you add a new loop, add its preview function first.

Never collapse user-created structure during reorganization.. Auto-classification does not override human-assigned types. Cluster-to-Notebook does not remove Objects from existing Notebooks. Edge decay does not touch human-created edges. User intent is sacred.

Never run unbounded loops.. Every loop must have a maximum iteration count or a convergence check. If entity promotion finds 500 candidates, process them in batches with progress tracking, not all at once.

Never create a loop without a Timeline event.. If the system changed structure, there must be a record of what changed, when, why, and what the state was before. This is non-negotiable for auditability.
Never add a loop without adding it to periodic_reorganize().. Orphan loops that run outside the orchestration framework are invisible and unmanageable.
Stack: spaCy + BM25 + TF-IDF + sklearn. No PyTorch. No FAISS. No SBERT.
Memory: ~512MB per gunicorn worker. spaCy en_core_web_md is ~40MB. Two workers = tight budget.
Constraint: Every feature must work in this mode, even if degraded. If a feature cannot degrade gracefully, it cannot ship.
Stack: All 7 engine passes. PyTorch + FAISS + SBERT + NLI + KGE.
Memory: Unconstrained (developer machine).
Purpose: Full-fidelity development and testing. Features are built here first, then degradation paths are added.
Stack: Batch re-encoding, KGE training, GNN training, LM fine-tuning, SAM-2 image analysis, corpus-wide NLI, evolutionary optimization.
Dispatch: Triggered from RQ tasks via httpx calls to Modal endpoints.
Purpose: Heavy computation that cannot run on Railway or even local dev in reasonable time.
Engine tasks are long-running and CPU-intensive. The 600s timeout accommodates full 7-pass enrichment on large Objects.
Ingestion tasks are I/O-bound (file reading, OCR, tree-sitter parsing). The 120s timeout prevents stuck jobs from blocking the queue.
Default queue handles everything else. Index rebuilds can be slow but are less time-critical.
config/: Django settings split by environment (base, development, production, modal). Production settings enforce the Railway constraints.
requirements/: Dependency files split by environment. base.txt has Railway-safe deps only. local.txt adds PyTorch, FAISS, sentence-transformers. modal.txt adds GPU-specific deps.

apps/notebook/tasks.py: RQ task definitions. Each task is decorated with its queue assignment and timeout. Contains the inline fallback pattern: if RQ is unavailable, tasks execute synchronously on the request path.
Thin handlers, thick tasks. API views validate input and dispatch to RQ. They do not run engine passes or file processing on the request path.
Explicit degradation status. When a feature runs in degraded mode, the API response includes a degraded: true flag and a degraded_reason string. The frontend can display this to users.
Memory-aware model loading. Models (spaCy, SBERT, etc.) are loaded once at module level and shared across requests. Never load a model inside a view function.
Queue dispatch with inline fallback. Every RQ task dispatch has a try/except that falls back to synchronous execution if Redis is unavailable. This prevents total failure when the queue is down.

Never break Railway by adding hard PyTorch runtime dependencies.. Every import of torch, transformers, sentence-transformers, or faiss must be wrapped in try/except with an _AVAILABLE flag. No exceptions.

Never run heavy model inference on the request path.. SBERT encoding, NLI scoring, KGE predictions, and image analysis go through RQ tasks or Modal dispatch. The request path returns in under 2 seconds.

Never merge architecture changes without operational checks.. Before any infrastructure change: verify it works with requirements/base.txt only (Railway simulation), verify RQ tasks still dispatch correctly, verify cache invalidation still fires.

Never add a new queue without justifying why the existing three are insufficient.. Queue proliferation creates operational complexity. If your task fits the timeout and purpose of an existing queue, use it.
Never bypass the inline fallback pattern.. Every RQ dispatch must handle the case where Redis is down. Synchronous fallback is the safety net.
Never hardcode environment-specific values.. Railway URLs, Modal endpoints, Redis connection strings, and worker counts come from environment variables, not settings files.
Consistency: The revised belief set must not contain contradictions
Minimal change: Revise as little as possible to accommodate new evidence
Recovery: If a belief is abandoned and the reason for abandonment is later retracted, the belief can be re-adopted
Level 4: Extend self_organize.py Loop 5 with RNNLogic-learned type inference rules
Level 7: tms.py (Truth Maintenance System over Claim justification networks)
Level 7: belief_revision.py (AGM-principled revision when contradictions arise)
Level 8: analogy_engine.py (structure-mapping across knowledge domains)
Cross-level: rules.py (defeasible rule engine via PyReason integration)
Never implement TMS without proper justification records.. Every Claim needs its support chain tracked.
Never apply belief revision without the minimal change principle.. Revise as little as possible.
Never auto-execute learned rules.. Rules are proposed by RNNLogic and reviewed before deployment (Invariant #7).
Never build ATMS for graphs with fewer than 50 Claims.. The overhead is not justified.
Never use analogy as evidence.. Analogies generate hypotheses, they do not confirm them.
A small increase in edge density can cause community structure to suddenly emerge (or collapse)
Crossing the entity promotion threshold can cause a cascade of promotions
Enabling two amplifying loops simultaneously can cause runaway behavior
Sensitivity scripts: management commands that measure parameter sensitivity
Dampening coefficients: every feedback loop has a configurable dampening factor
Loop activation flags: per-Notebook engine_config controls which loops are active
Monitoring: IQ Tracker variance alerts for loop instability
Never enable two new feedback loops simultaneously.. One at a time, with monitoring between.
Never skip sensitivity analysis before closing a loop.. The 10%/30% test is mandatory.
Never let positive feedback run without dampening.. Every amplifying loop needs a ceiling or decay counterpart.
Never assume stability.. Complex systems can appear stable for weeks and then suddenly destabilize. Continuous monitoring is required.
Never optimize a single axis at the expense of system stability.. IQ composite matters more than any individual axis.
Object capture (node creation)
Edge creation/deletion (structural change)
User clicks/navigation (engagement)
Engine runs (processing)
Object edits (content change)
Travis fork: refs/tgn/ (already exists at Travis-Gilbert/Temporal-Graph-Networks_tgn)
New file: temporal_memory.py (Modal GPU job)
Existing: temporal_evolution.py (bridges to new temporal memory)
Integration: temporal memory vectors as features in learned scorer
Never shuffle temporal data.. Training must respect chronological order. Random shuffling leaks future information.
Never use TGN on graphs with fewer than 1,000 events.. The memory module needs sufficient history to learn meaningful patterns.
Never replace exponential decay entirely until TGN is validated.. Run both in parallel, compare via IQ Tracker, and switch only when TGN demonstrably outperforms.
Never train TGN in production.. Modal GPU only.
refs/index-api/apps/notebook/temporal_evolution.py for existing temporal logic
Edge strength > 0.7 between two Objects indicates semantic relatedness.
The anchor is one Object's text, the positive is the connected Object's text.
Edge type provides context: a "supports" edge creates a different training signal than a "contradicts" edge.
Objects in different Louvain communities with no connecting edges are candidate negatives.
Hard negatives are more valuable than random negatives: they are semantically plausible but not actually related.
Mine hard negatives by finding Objects with high BM25 similarity but no edges (lexically similar, semantically unrelated).
Random negatives: easy to mine, low training signal.
In-batch negatives: other positives in the same batch serve as negatives. Efficient but can introduce false negatives if the batch contains semantically related pairs.
Hard negatives from community structure: highest training signal, requires graph analysis.
Identify claim pairs near the NLI decision boundary (entailment score between 0.4 and 0.6).
Present these uncertain pairs to users for confirmation or correction.
User feedback on boundary cases provides maximum information gain per label.
Track which examples the model is most uncertain about and prioritize those for human review.
Start with a pretrained SBERT model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2).
Fine-tune on domain-specific triplets constructed from the user's corpus.
Use contrastive learning (MultipleNegativesRankingLoss) for retrieval improvement.
Evaluate before and after on held-out retrieval tasks to confirm improvement.
Re-encode the full corpus with the fine-tuned model and rebuild FAISS indexes.
Precision@k: Of the top k results, how many are relevant?
Recall@k: Of all relevant results, how many appear in the top k?
MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): How far down the list is the first relevant result?
NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain): Rewards relevant results appearing higher in the ranking.
Claim-level F1: Precision and recall on entailment/contradiction/neutral classification.
Calibration: Does a 70% confidence prediction actually mean 70% accuracy?
Edge prediction accuracy: Can the model predict held-out edges from the graph?
Link prediction: Given (subject, relation, ?), does the model rank the correct object highly?

Retrieval learning. : Improve BM25 and SBERT from user engagement signals (which results did users click, confirm, or dismiss). This has the highest immediate impact because retrieval quality affects everything downstream.

Knowledge learning. : Build a world model from user review and revision patterns. When users edit auto-generated edges or reclassify objects, that is supervision signal for the knowledge representation.
Model training. : Fine-tune extraction and classification models (NER, claim decomposition, ObjectType classification) using the accumulated labeled data from steps 1 and 2.
Format: (sha_hash_1, edge_type, sha_hash_2)
Source: Edge model in models.py
Signal: Edge strength (>0.7 = positive pair), edge type (semantic relationship type), user-confirmed vs. auto-generated
Format: (claim_text_1, claim_text_2, nli_label)
Source: Claim decomposition (Pass 6 of engine.py) + NLI scoring
Format: (object_sha, action, timestamp)
Source: Timeline events, UI interaction logs
Signal: Confirmed edges (positive), dismissed edges (negative), ignored edges (uncertain)
Format: (object_sha, community_id)
Source: Louvain community detection in community.py
Signal: Weak supervision -- objects in the same community are likely related

refs/sentence-transformers/: Source code for the sentence-transformers library. Read this for fine-tuning API, loss functions (MultipleNegativesRankingLoss, CosineSimilarityLoss), and evaluation (InformationRetrievalEvaluator).
refs/research_api/scripts/train_kge.py: Knowledge graph embedding training script. Uses PyKEEN or custom training loop for TransE/DistMult/ComplEx models.
Training runs on Modal (GPU). Dispatched via httpx from RQ tasks.
Training data is extracted and serialized on Railway/local, then sent to Modal for the actual training loop.
Trained models are stored and versioned. The production system loads the latest model on startup (if available).
FAISS indexes must be rebuilt after any embedding model update. This is a separate RQ task.
Never train without a held-out evaluation set.. Every training run must reserve data for evaluation. Lower training loss without evaluation on held-out data proves nothing.
Never deploy a fine-tuned model without comparing to the baseline.. Measure retrieval quality (Precision@k, MRR) before and after. If the fine-tuned model is not measurably better, do not deploy it.

Never construct training data that leaks evaluation data.. If an edge is in the evaluation set, neither of its endpoint Objects can appear in training triplets. Leakage produces artificially high metrics.
Never fine-tune on Railway.. Training happens on Modal (GPU) or local dev. Railway does not have the memory or compute for training loops. The training pipeline dispatches to Modal via httpx.

Never rebuild FAISS indexes during peak usage.. Index rebuilds are memory-intensive. Schedule them during off-peak hours or on Modal. The old index continues serving queries until the new one is ready.

Never discard training provenance.. Every training run records: which data was used, which model version was produced, what the evaluation metrics were, and when it ran. This is the empirical record of model evolution.
Strip navigation, ads, sidebars, and boilerplate. The goal is the article body, not the page chrome.
Firecrawl outputs clean markdown from web pages. This is the preferred extraction method for URLs.
Preserve document structure (headings, lists, tables, code blocks) because structure carries semantic information.
Handle failure gracefully: paywalls, 404s, rate limits, and bot detection are normal, not exceptional.
Claim extraction. : Decompose articles into sentence-sized propositions (claims).
Entity co-occurrence. : Track which entities appear together across sources. Co-occurrence without explicit edges suggests potential connections.
Contradiction pairs. : Compare editorial sources against research sources. Disagreements between them produce high-value NLI training pairs.
Paywalls: Detect paywall markers in extracted content. Set scrape_blocked=True on the Object. Do not store partial content as if it were complete.
404s and dead links: Record the failure in the Object metadata. Do not retry immediately -- use exponential backoff.
Rate limiting: Respect robots.txt and rate limits. Queue requests with configurable delay between them.
Retry strategy: Failed captures go back into the RQ ingestion queue with exponential backoff (1min, 5min, 30min, 2hr). After 4 failures, mark as permanently failed.
PDF: Text extraction with section detection. Falls back to OCR for scanned documents.
DOCX: python-docx extracts text, tables, and metadata.
PPTX: python-pptx extracts slide text and speaker notes.
XLSX: openpyxl extracts cell data and sheet structure.
Images: OCR for text extraction. SAM-2 (Segment Anything Model 2) via Modal for image segmentation and region labeling. SAM-2 runs on Modal GPU, not locally.
Code files: tree-sitter AST parsing extracts symbol definitions, imports, and documentation strings. Produces a structured representation of the code's architecture.
Plain text and Markdown: Direct ingestion with minimal preprocessing.
URL enrichment: Fetches OpenGraph metadata (og_title, og_description, og_image, og_site_name) to populate Object fields before content extraction.
RQ dispatch: Capture tasks are dispatched to the ingestion queue (120s timeout). If RQ is unavailable, falls back to inline synchronous execution.
Object creation: Creates an Object with URL, OG metadata, and extracted content. The Object's url field is the canonical source reference.
url: The source URL (canonical reference).
og_title: OpenGraph title from the source page.
og_description: OpenGraph description.
og_image: OpenGraph image URL.
og_site_name: The site's name (e.g., "Nature", "ArXiv", "GitHub").
Scrape URLs via Firecrawl. : Send URLs to Firecrawl API, receive clean markdown. Firecrawl handles JavaScript rendering, cookie walls, and boilerplate removal.
Decompose claims from scraped content. : Run claim decomposition on the extracted text. Each article produces multiple sentence-sized claims.
Run NLI on claim pairs across sources. : Compare claims from different sources using Natural Language Inference. Entailment = corroboration. Contradiction = tension. Neutral = independent.

Construct training triplets from high-confidence pairs. : Claim pairs with high NLI confidence (>0.8) become training data. Entailment pairs become positive examples. Contradiction pairs with high confidence become hard negatives.
Fine-tune SBERT on domain-specific triplets. : Use the constructed triplets to fine-tune the retrieval model. This runs on Modal GPU.
Rebuild FAISS index with new embeddings. : Re-encode the full corpus with the fine-tuned model. Rebuild the FAISS index. This also runs on Modal.
Evaluate retrieval quality improvement. : Compare Precision@k, MRR, and NDCG before and after fine-tuning on a held-out evaluation set. Only deploy the new model if metrics improve.
refs/firecrawl/: Firecrawl library source. Read for API patterns, rate limiting, and output format.
refs/research_api/apps/notebook/file_ingestion.py: The actual file ingestion implementation. Read for supported formats, extraction strategies, and error handling patterns.

Never store partial paywall content as complete.. If extraction detects paywall markers (truncated text, "subscribe to read more", login walls), set scrape_blocked=True and do not run claim extraction on the partial content. Partial content produces misleading claims.
Never scrape without rate limiting.. Every external request goes through the rate limiter. Respect robots.txt. Do not parallelize requests to the same domain beyond what the site allows.

Never skip OG metadata enrichment.. URL captures must attempt OpenGraph metadata extraction before content extraction. OG metadata provides the Object's title, description, and site attribution even if content extraction fails.
Never run SAM-2 or heavy OCR on Railway.. Image analysis tasks dispatch to Modal via httpx. The Railway worker does not have the memory for SAM-2 or GPU for efficient OCR on large documents.

Never treat extraction failure as data loss.. A failed extraction produces an Object with metadata (URL, OG fields, timestamps) but no content. The Object exists as a placeholder that can be re-extracted later. Do not silently drop failed captures.

Never construct training data from unverified extractions.. The Firecrawl pipeline produces training data. If the extraction quality is poor (truncated, garbled, wrong language), the training data will be poor. Validate extraction quality before feeding into triplet construction.
PRODUCTION (Railway): spaCy + BM25 + TF-IDF. No PyTorch.
LOCAL/DEV: All 7+ passes. PyTorch + FAISS + SBERT + NLI + KGE.
MODAL (GPU batch): GNN training, KGE training, LoRA fine-tuning,
Patterns: patterns/PATTERNS-*.md for implementation recipes.
Product: product/*.md for specs, roadmap, and design docs.
patterns/ has 17 executable pattern documents
product/ has roadmap, specs, and landscape analysis
examples/ has runnable code samples per workflow
scripts/bootstrap_refs.sh clones all 43 reference repos
Note which claims you consult and why
Note suggestion outcomes (accepted, modified, rejected)
Note patterns not yet in the knowledge base
Note any corrections the user makes that contradict existing claims
Filename: [domain-slug]-[YYYY-MM-DD].md
Format: Problem, Root Cause, Solution, Prevention, Claims Extracted
Keep it concise. 10-30 lines total.
A single imperative statement (starts with a verb or "always/never")
Scoped to one actionable practice
Tagged with the relevant domain from the agent domain map
Use difference-preserving aggregation for opposition edges in the GNN, not standard mean aggregation
Precompute Ollivier-Ricci Curvature via Leiden community detection, never learn it end-to-end during GNN training
Update the epistemic embedding channel only from agreement edges; never propagate contradiction through the epistemic state
Export h_content and h_epistemic as separate vectors for GL-Fusion Stream A
Measure contradiction separation as >= 0.10 cosine similarity reduction between contradicting nodes versus base R-GCN
Filter relations with fewer than 10 instances before KGE training; sparse relations produce noise
Materialize inverse triples for symmetric relations when training RotatE
Use neighbor-averaged embeddings for cold-start KGE prediction on new Objects
Pre-compute KGE structural tokens before the LLM generation loop, never during
Mine hard negatives from same Louvain community with no connecting edge for SBERT fine-tuning
Use MultipleNegativesRankingLoss for SBERT fine-tuning because in-batch negatives scale with batch size
Freeze both SBERT and GNN encoders during contrastive alignment; train only the projection heads
Require cross-modal cosine similarity above 0.4 and overlap at 10 above 40 percent as quality gate for graph-text alignment
Use -1.0 imputation for all KGE features when the model is unavailable, never 0.0
Drop any KGE scorer feature with Shapley importance below 0.01
Add alignment loss alpha equals 0.1 between GNN and frozen KGE embeddings during co-training
Never run GL-Fusion cross-attention on CPU; GGUF cannot represent custom architecture layers
Require fusion gate alpha standard deviation above 0.05 across query types to confirm the gate is discriminating
Skip auto-capture for trivial typo fixes, one-line config changes, and obvious missing dependencies
Claims with confidence above 0.8 override static instructions when conflicting
Never use the 4B agent-tier model for user-facing synthesis; the quality gap is immediately noticeable
Route all external web content through the Object ingestion pipeline before LLMs see it
Never auto-promote high-confidence claims to the shared graph; promotion requires explicit human action
Preserve Tensions in the shared graph with their full history; never merge contradicting claims during deduplication

SFT label setup must mask prompt tokens with -100. Tokenize prompt separately for prompt_len, then labels[:, :prompt_len] = -100 AND labels[labels == pad_id] = -100. Without prompt mask, model learns echo-mode: reproduces prompt in output.

Gemma 4 decoder layers (transformers >=4.57) hard-require position_embeddings (tuple of cos/sin, per-layer-type via layer_types config), shared_kv_states (dict), and mm_token_type_ids in train mode when Gemma4ForConditionalGeneration.forward is invoked.

PEFT DoRA materializes full [out, in] weight tensor at every forward via weight + scaling * lora_weight. For Gemma 4 31B all-linear (60 layers × 7 modules), intermediate weight tensors accumulate to ~50 GB per forward. Major memory cost not estimated in V4 spec.

HyperbolicMessagePassing einsum('rb,bio->rio', comps, bases) materializes a [num_rel, D, D] = 1.96 GB tensor at num_rel=34, D=5376, bf16. Factored form (sum_b comps[r, b] * (src @ bases[b])) drops peak intermediate to tens of KB.

torch.utils.checkpoint(use_reentrant=False) does strict metadata validation on recomputed tensors. Modules with save-order-dependent gates or non-deterministic forward raise CheckpointError on recompute. Drop the manual wrap when memory pressure is already solved by other means.

FSDP2 fully_shard is bottom-up: apply to decoder layers first, then the root. Per-layer shards without a root coordinator produce orphan FSDP units that don't coordinate gather/release — each rank holds full model replica.

FSDP2 fully_shard on base_model makes embedding.weight a DTensor. Custom wrappers calling get_input_embeddings()(input_ids) with local-tensor input raise aten.embedding.default mixed-tensor error. Fix: register_fsdp_forward_method, or explicit root unshard, or use device_map='auto' (pipeline parallelism) instead.

PEFT init_lora_weights='eva' installs activation-capture hooks on every LoRA Linear. If EVA calibration fails (e.g., CPU/FSDP device bug), hooks persist and crash on every subsequent forward with distributed all-gather errors. Drop EVA init entirely when calibration isn't feasible.

HuggingFace device_map='auto' for Gemma 4 31B uses sequential filling (cuda:0 first), overloading cuda:0. 'balanced' skews similarly. 'balanced_low_0' puts everything on cuda:1. max_memory={i: 'XGiB'} forces even distribution but must exceed half-model-size to avoid meta-device offload.

When debugging OOMs, iterating fixes without instrumentation is wasteful. After 3 failures with same error signature, stop and add torch.cuda.memory_summary() / memory_snapshot() rather than guessing. Memory category breakdown (weights, activations, workspace) pinpoints the consumer in minutes.

Pre-training verifier must exercise backward pass under the actual multi-GPU config. Forward-only passing misses: labels bugs (echo-mode), memory ceiling during backward, gradient_checkpointing mis-wiring, autograd version issues from in-place ops. Backward smoke test on minimum-viable inputs is essential.

HF's gradient_checkpointing_enable() configures decoder layers to self-checkpoint internally via self._gradient_checkpointing_func. Custom wrappers that call decoder_layer() directly still benefit automatically. Manual wrapping in torch.utils.checkpoint on top double-wraps, adds memory, and breaks metadata validation.

Stage 1 contrastive training does NOT train LoRA adapters — base_model forward is in torch.no_grad() for text encoding, so only gnn_projector receives gradients. After Stage 1, LoRA weights equal init. Resume logic: LoRA state in after-contrastive is equivalent to fresh PEFT init.
labels = input_ids.clone() without prompt masking is an SFT anti-pattern

evidence.grounding: V1 produced echo-mode model despite loss 0.85 + gates 10/16

text: Always --detach any Modal run >5 min

evidence.grounding: session disconnect killed labeler at 10K/11500, lost $14 DeepSeek + 100 min

text: Incremental S3 checkpointing every N rows for batch labeling jobs

evidence.grounding: RAM-only result accumulation risks total loss on any crash

text: PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True for variable-length training

evidence.grounding: OOM at step 500 from fragmentation; fixed with env var

text: Modal env() must come before add_local_python_source()

evidence.grounding: Modal explicitly rejected reverse order with helpful error

text: Verify artifacts on S3 before claiming outcome (won, lost, crashed)

evidence.grounding: twice claimed false outcomes (data loss, embed restoration) by reading logs instead of artifacts

text: Forward-pass verifier must also run backward smoke test

evidence.grounding: 3 of 9 forward/backward crashes (autograd version, dtype promotion, scatter_reduce) would have been caught by loss.backward() on dummy batch

text: Read existing infrastructure before designing new pipelines

evidence.grounding: proposed 5-stage teacher pipeline when deepseek_dispatch.py + DeepSeekTrace already existed for exactly this purpose

text: Identical eval metrics across multiple checkpoints suggest bug, not convergence

evidence.grounding: completion_ppl=1.98 repeated exactly at steps 1000/2000/3000/4000 is too clean; signals eval computation issue OR local-minimum attractor

text: Gate opening rate is not a sufficient SFT quality signal

evidence.grounding: V1 reached 10/16 gates with loss 0.85 but produced echo-mode at inference; gate count without output-quality signal can mislead

text: Shared-primitive latent bugs surface when dtype changes across consumers

evidence.grounding: _aggregate msgs+counts default float32 worked in 31B (Gemma float32) but broke 14B (Qwen bf16); buffer dtype should track input dtype universally

text: Teacher-labeled SFT corpora need output variance to avoid early plateau

evidence.grounding: DeepSeek-R1 produced structurally uniform JSON outputs; model reached PPL 1.98 in 1000 steps and plateaued for 3000+ more

text: 31B trainer has same full-sequence-labels anti-pattern as 14B V1

evidence.grounding: train_true_gl_fusion.py line 1030 uses same labels=input_ids.clone() pattern; will produce same echo-mode failure if trained without masking fix

text: Phase 0 verifier MUST exercise loss.backward + optimizer.step + post-step forward; forward-only smoke tests pass even when generation/training paths are broken (V5 caught optimizer-step interactions forward-only would miss).
GPT-5.x reasoning models reject `max_tokens` parameter; use `max_completion_tokens` instead. 400 BadRequestError otherwise. Old `max_tokens` still works for GPT-4 / DeepSeek / older OpenAI models.

Local Django mgmt commands against Railway PG die with `OperationalError: terminating connection due to administrator command` on long sessions. Pattern: chunk in batches of ~500, call `from django.db import connection; connection.close()` between batches, use --append flag for accumulating S3 outputs.

Tigris/Railway S3 requires explicit endpoint_url in boto3 client. Only pass when AWS_S3_ENDPOINT_URL env var is non-empty (empty string breaks boto3). Pattern: kwargs={}; if endpoint_url: kwargs['endpoint_url']=endpoint_url; boto3.client('s3', **kwargs).

GL-Fusion corpus extraction must follow Stream A export, not precede it. If corpus PKs > Stream A's max PK, _build_graph_data returns None for all rows → augmentation pool fallback → real_pct=0% → text-only fine-tune (gates never open). V5 lost 95% of Cat 1/2/6 to this.

Stream A retrain only re-trains EpiGNN weights on existing topology. To pick up new Object PKs, first re-export entity_map.tsv + triples.tsv from Django, THEN retrain. Otherwise gnn-export's max PK stays stale.

GL-Fusion zero-init gates need cold-start intervention. With spec §4.3 zero init + standard graph LR (5e-5), gates stay frozen at ~0.002 (10× below spec §5.3 minimum 0.02) even with 55%+ real_pct. Chicken-and-egg failure. Fix candidates: gate-specific high LR (10×), init gates at 0.01, two-phase optimizer.
Modal `app stop` on ephemeral apps may cancel subsequent same-named dispatches. Wait briefly between stop and re-dispatch, or use distinct app names per dispatch.

Modal `.map()` worker preemption can kill orchestrator functions silently mid-iteration, leaving partial S3 state. Always pair `.map()` orchestrators with explicit resume logic that reads existing output AND a watchdog that re-dispatches if orchestrator dies.

Phase 0/1/2 GL-Fusion verifiers exercise forward + loss.backward only — never model.generate(). Inference path can be completely broken even when training metrics look healthy. Run a separate inference smoke test against the base model BEFORE training.

google/gemma-4-31B-it cannot generate text via transformers.generate() on this stack. Tested all of: attn_implementation eager AND sdpa, greedy AND temperature sampling, repetition_penalty, apply_chat_template, manual <start_of_turn> formatting, mm_token_type_ids passthrough, base.model.language_model.generate (which doesn't exist on Gemma4TextModel). All produce gibberish. Model only generates via vLLM (multimodal-Gemma-specific kernels).

GNN store's runtime dim is data-determined. Stream A base is 160d (h_content 128 + h_epistemic 32) but grows to 416d when GeoMoE + TGN variants exist on S3. Inference wrappers that hardcode gnn_dim=160 will fail to load 416d-trained checkpoints with shape mismatch on gnn_projector.

Gemma 4 31B-IT transformers.generate requires do_sample=True + top_p=0.95 + top_k=64 to avoid argmax loops. Greedy decoding (do_sample=False) produces degenerate single-token repetition regardless of wrapper or loader code. Model default generation_config declares sampling; pipelines and chat APIs all use it.
source: canary run 9 baseline diagnostic + vanilla AutoModelForCausalLM.generate

text: Canary decode checks on LLM wrappers must use token-level coherence: len(set(new_token_ids))/len(new_token_ids) >= 0.3 AND bigram diversity >= 0.4. Char-level heuristics miss CJK collapse because dense scripts emit one character per token, producing 16-char strings that pass any length-based repetition gate.
source: canary runs 5-8 CJK collapse detection failures

text: HF from_pretrained auto-prefix-stripper does not bridge multimodal model.language_model.* to text-only model.* for Gemma 4. Load via AutoModelForCausalLM (resolves to Gemma4ForConditionalGeneration, loads weights correctly, wrapper bypasses multimodal via direct decoder iteration) OR explicit state-dict remap + tie_weights() call after _from_config.
source: canary runs 1-2 (silent all-MISSING/UNEXPECTED report), canary 7 state-dict remap diagnostic

text: Lorentz hyperboloid ops need both fp32 boundaries AND output magnitude clamping. Fp32 loses the +1 in sqrt(1+space_norm_sq) once space_norm_sq exceeds ~10^7 (7-digit mantissa). Clamp ||space|| <= 15 at HTC output, run expmap/logmap/project/centroid/distance in fp32 with cast back at the boundary. bf16 cancellation is a separate failure mode that fp32-only ops avoid.

GenerationMixin-aware wrappers need: main_input_name class attr, self.config, self.generation_config, @property device, @property dtype, get_input_embeddings and set/output variants, prepare_inputs_for_generation, generate override, can_generate returning True, ModelOutput subclass as forward return (NOT plain dict), and __getattr__ fallthrough to base_model for HF-specific attrs (set_experts_implementation, hf_device_map, _supports_sdpa) that transformers reads from the outer model.
source: canary runs 3-5 AttributeError chain on TrueGLFusionGemma wrapper

text: Teacher-forced training (forward with labels) is independent of generate path quality. Even if wrapper.generate produces gibberish, training still produces correct weights. Inference-time generate quality depends only on the sampling params the caller supplies. Do not block training on inference-config bugs.
source: session reasoning about V6 failure modes post canary 6

text: lorentz_expmap0 MUST separate the unit direction from the clamped scaled norm. space = sinh(sc_vn) * v_unit / sqrt(c), NOT sinh(sc_vn) * v / sc_vn. The latter loses hyperboloid closure when ||v|| > EXP_CLAMP_NORM because v / sc_vn is no longer unit-length. Handoff proof: with unit separation, <x,x>_L = -cosh^2(sc_vn) + sinh^2(sc_vn) * ||v_unit||^2 = -1 exactly regardless of clamp.
source: canary run 3 Lorentz invariant 4.6e10 drift on 5376-dim random input

text: FlamingoGate (scalar tanh(alpha) with alpha=Parameter(zeros(1))) replaces sigmoid-matrix FusionGate because sigmoid(0)=0.5 starts both streams at half-power damaging perplexity from step 0. tanh(0)=0 exactly closes the branch; sech^2(0)=1 keeps gradient non-vanishing. Combined with gate-specific 10x LR and link-prediction aux loss, this breaks the chicken-and-egg where gates cannot open until useful and cannot be useful until open.
source: V6 HANDOFF research synthesis + canary run 5 gate_zero_invariance_check PASS
